turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- all I'm saying is that the case is not disposable by that kind of logical reasoning.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I think, perhaps so, Your Honor. But and may I respectfully submit two points in answer to that. And that is, one, that if this right was assignable, this power, did the present instrument accomplish that at all (Inaudible) required by contract to agree that he will leave a will, naming an executor authorize the executor, renew the copyright and transfer it to his assignee. He agrees to bequeath and it's a separate assignable right. He agrees to transfer his right if he lives and he agrees to bequeath if he dies. And the second answer to that if I may respectfully submit, Mr. Justice Frankfurter, is that in this case, we only mentioned the point because under the theory of the District Court, he rejected our California argument as I -- I call it. His -- he said, “The author's death prior to the 28th year met the -- the assignment in this case conveyed nothing because the author died and that the people who are the legatees therefore, get the right that the executor had, are not bound by that prior assignment.”

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Am I to infer from what you said that the -- that the author could make a contract binding his executor with the creation of a right for specific performance by the assignee so far?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: No. I did not -- I did not submit specific performance in my statement.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, but by specific performance I mean that he could get a renewal that the executor would have to in his designated capacity as for the renewal and then transfer it, that's was I loosely called --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I'd like to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- in effect, specific performance.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- I'd like to answer that in three steps. Yes, under the copyright law that it would be possible, two, you'd have to refer to the state law to see if the state law will permit it. I submit, under the authority of De Sylva-Ballentine that once you have --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That question isn't here?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: That question isn't here though, Your Honor. That's the point. Moreover even --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All I'm saying is, I'm -- I'm interested in your argument that this could be accomplished by making an arrangement using a neutral term, an arrangement in the light of the author who predeceases his 28th year and yet have to be effectuated by the executor so that the assignee have to almost get the reality, namely the assignment.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: If -- if under particular state law he was able to buy the right to -- to inherit by will, if he was -- I could go to a man and say, “I will give you” and it's done. Let's give the typical example of some of these annuity policies or all the folk songs, where they go to someone who say, “You turn over all of your assets or you give us $10,000 or if we renamed as his beneficiary of your -- of Black then -- then we will support you for the rest of your life.” It's an agreement to live a will. The California law, if certain formalities are observed, it is a proper agreement.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I should think it raises a number -- created a number of questions that we did not decide or face in De Sylva that you could so construe the copyright law that you could work out an assignment that after the author's death before his 28th year in some states not in other, that would give me a lot of trouble myself.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, perhaps so and perhaps it should be a matter of state law -- a -- a federal law that is not assignable.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, we -- we have to catch all that out of the air in dealing with the question whether an author who dies before the 28th year can effectuate the contingency to fall into the hands of his or for shorthand called assignee.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: It might be the next case this Court would have to decide --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- it's not present in this case, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No. But --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, is this case on all force with Fisher in your judgment?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Some all force with respect to the fact that in the Fisher case, this Court simply held that there was no restriction on the assignability of the future contingent right that a man could assign his renewal right prior to the time that vested.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: But the Fisher case went on to recognize that if he died prior to the right vesting, the assignee took exactly nothing because the right didn't vest. That's as far as the Fisher case went.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The Fisher case decide this case by dictum?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: No, Your Honor. I don't -- I don't think the Fisher --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Closely reprehensible then.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: No. I don't think -- yes, it would -- it would be but I'm afraid it was the -- I believe my recollection was the -- the dictum but --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I take it back. Your Honor showed greater care of than the Court of Appeals. There was the argument --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I had hoped so in that --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes. I apologize. [Laughter] The brief -- the brief did argue -- the brief of the respondent did argue. Of course, if the author assigns the renewal term but does not survive, the assignee's whose rights cannot be greater than the author's will take nothing. That statement also appeared in the Court of Appeals decisions, my apologies, did not appear in your opinions, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Did I -- did I --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I assumed it was -- it -- it has been so often quoted as having appeared in that opinion that I was almost made to believe.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Very responsible for all the misquotations by even eminent members of the bar. I see even less that, worst -- worst and argue about my performances.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: But -- but be that as it may, the Fisher-Witmark case did assign that it was -- I feel cited only one question that it could be assigned prior to its vesting. And that was the only (Voice Overlap) Fisher-Witmark case.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) you say while ago but it's made and he has a wife really. What did I understand you to say about that?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: If he has a -- if he has a wife --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And what about the assignment if he has a wife then?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, he would have to get it from the wife because if the wife would then get it, wife or children.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And then the children.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, not in then -- I'm sorry, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Not -- not in then, the wife and children in the same class.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now -- -- I'm -- I'm not talking -- (Voice Overlap) I'm not talking how they do it.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: For the last few years my -- I -- I must have --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: For getting --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- hope it to remain the same.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I want to move on -- definitely that deals because I don't quite understand your distinction. Yes, do you say that the wife and children and what about the next of kin when you go down and he has no will?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Only if he fails to leave a will.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Alright. He fails to leave a will.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Then the next of kin would take it.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: They'd have to get it. Is the assignment good against them?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: No, it would not be.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Not good against them.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: No. And I think, the petitioner --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Is it any different?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- is here to concede that he says he protected himself by getting an assignment from the next of kin.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Then we end up that way.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: No. It's not here.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- because of here here.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Is there a difference to be (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And there we have Fisher either, did you?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: For -- for me --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Is there any difference in that in practical effect in the claim of the executor?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: No. I don't think there's any difference. I think that --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: The claim of the executor of course would be for whoever the beneficiaries are.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I believe the executor represents the first of the beneficiaries in this case and not the person of the testator. That's the difference. I think if I -- Mr. Justice Frankfurter, said in question, the executor is a legal institution, he must represent something. You can only get what the decedent had. And I differ in that respect to some extent, Mr. Justice Frankfurter because -- let's take a common situation of a power of appointment in the will where a man is given the life interest but no right to invade the corpus. But it is provided that he may on his death, by will, executed the due formalities of the state in which he lives, exercise a power of appointment to distribute the corpus of the trust and direct his executor to do so or should go to his executor for that purpose. Or he might under some constructions and under some trust even exercise it to become part of the general assets of his -- his estate. It's a very common thing these days. We've had with men who put their assets in the hands of a bank, say even in a irrevocable inter vivos trust to provide for a power of appointment he exercised by will and he exercise that power of appointment in favor of their executors for the purposes set forth in the will.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you one other --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Might be (Voice Overlap) purposes.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- other question, I don't get quite understand. Do you draw, I understand, or does the other side draw any distinction between the right of the next of kin and the executor?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes. I believe they do, they must. They draw a distinction between the right of the next of kin and the right of widow or children and the executor. They say, that they recognize that if the author's inter vivos assignment, the author's assignment tenure's life prior to the 28th year, and if he dies prior to the 28th year, does not defeat the rights of the widow, widower or children but defeats the -- or the next of kin.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Or the next of kin?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Or the next of kin. We concede that.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well -- well, do I understand then that they say that it doesn't defeat the right of the next of kin? They can get it if he makes no will despite the assignment.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: If he makes a will, he somehow cuts off the beneficiaries even if they are the next of kin?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes, because here in this particular case, they had an assignment in the next of kin and they are not asserting their rights under that assignment.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But -- but you don't -- in your view doesn't give anything to the next of kin, does it?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: In my view in -- in what under the facts stated by, Mr. Justice Black.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No. Your -- on -- in this case?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: --Because -- because the -- this -- because the testamentary disposition deter -- controls the whole disposition.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Steps three came in beforehand, yes Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well that, suppose he had made his next of kin the beneficiary.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I believe that there's --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Would that make any difference?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I don't -- it might have made a difference but not as a matter of copyright law. It would've made a difference based on the instrument, the matter of general law interpreting --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I'm talking about --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- the instrument.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I'm talking about this law.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: But under this law, I don't think it would make any difference.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: If it gives the right -- what I'm -- I -- I couldn't quite understand if --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: But they wouldn't take his next of kin.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: They wouldn't --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: If --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: That's the point.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: If the next of kin by reason of this statute cannot be cutoff by the assignment, why can those be cutoff from the executor, represents as the executor?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, I -- because --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What is the argument?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Because Congress could've well said that in the absence of a will, it should go to the Library of Congress for the benefit of buying books or general fund or for charity. Instead, Congress passed it on to the next of kin only, but only in the event. The author did not exercise his testamentary power.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But what about the executor, they only passed it to him if -- if he did exercise his testamentary --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I believe they passed it to the executor in his capacity, his illegal institution not beneficially. I agree that he did not get it beneficially. In his capacity as a legal institution as Mr. Justice Frankfurter put it but not as legal institution representing in this case, the person of his testator but as a legal institution representing the rights of the legatees under the will.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: You would have to make some distinction that's in light of the fact you don't get. You say that those two, the executor here, under the exercise by the testator of a power of appointment.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now, isn't that the mere name, you have a right to any -- any executor's requirements field, the exercise of the power of appointment in the sense you used that term.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes. It's a right. My --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: From any executor.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: They --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And that was --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Name an executor and under the (Inaudible) case to designate who shall receive the benefits of that.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, of course the executor appears on the will -- will --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- and with the power of appointment, the sanction here is that power to name (Inaudible)

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: That -- that's right.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now, you mean that true, in just every case with an executor's name?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: What's the difference between this and in the other (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, let me -- let me illustrate it directly with copyright law, Mr. Justice Whitaker. Let us assume in this case, “Moonlight and Roses”, the man or any copyright. The man has no widow or children and the copyright is in its 10th year of the original term and he owns the copyright, he's publishing it himself. He leaves to his executor the remaining 18 years of the original term. He lives it to his executor, the same manner that he could have assigned it to him. And the executor takes that right in the same manner that he might also take Black Acre or 100 shares of General Motors at the (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Any asset.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Any asset.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Revisable asset?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Right. But the asset involved here is not one that the man owned. It's a privilege extended by Congress.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But -- but isn't -- isn't that what the whole litigation is about?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, I at least --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- maybe what you just said. Namely, what it is that his got and what it is he can dispose of? We have to decide that before we get into all these other --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Correct. I --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- discussions.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: And we respectfully submit that if Congress, if you look at the purpose, if -- if you have the widow and children first, why did the Congress say, executor and not at -- to his estate or not to his executor's administrators or assigns if they intended that the third class as I put it, was of the same nature of the first class that the only thing that stopped it from merging together was the second class, the widow or children. I submit -- I admit the Court has to decide it, that's why we're here but I respectfully urge upon the Court that the construction must be that because of the intervening class and because of the schedule of rights.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I -- I must say I can -- I can understand why you're here and the argument your making but I can't understand that I'm on the logic of compulsion to hold that because Congress said we are going to protect the man's wife and his children, but we also give him the right to cut them off if he wants that and we also give him the right respecting that to make such other disposition as he wants to make, cut out all his next of kin and everybody else. That's true as far as --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I respectfully submit that is the position.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Congress gave him two rights in Section 24.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But It doesn't -- from that I can't -- you draw -- you say I'm on the logic of compulsion therefore to hold, that he cannot limit the -- that (Inaudible) power, the power to leave things by will or his protecting the wife and children. He cannot withdraw or limit or deprive himself of that by selling it from -- with cash in his lifetime.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: That's not quite what I've said, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I didn't say you said that. And you say practically, --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I thought -- I would like --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I did understand you to say that because of one and two, that sheds light on three, is that right?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I said, one and two would shed light if three is a different animal than one.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) The three is a different animal. The three -- three is a power, one is a -- is a grant of the direct right and I urged and I feel that it is not before this Court to decide whether he may convey one and three. There are two rights given to the author and step one, to take the copyright himself if he survives the 28th year and step three, that if he doesn't survive, it doesn't have a widow of children to intervene, that bequeath by will. I submit that both rights under the logic of Fisher versus Witmark, if one right is assignable, perhaps the other should be assignable but the Court need not decide that in this case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I agree with you. I quite agree with you.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: But right, we submit that rights --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That I also agree with you that I mustn't decide it your way.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: No. [Laughs] I appreciate that but I submit Your Honor that right one and three being different. And the Court, I submit looks to the instrument under which the appellant claims here and he sees that he acquires only right one that he did not bargain for nor obtain right three.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that's an -- that's a very different argument. That would -- they are different aspect of your position, namely that he really -- he could have assigned -- he could have effectuated in his lifetime. He get the power of renewal after 28 years although he is dead in the meantime.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes. He could.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That argument, as he could've but you say he didn't do it.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I say he did not --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.(Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- do it under the facts in this case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is totally different --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- but they carry your -- that they feel but all I'm saying that that does start with the proposition with the recognition that he could in his lifetime. Not reaching the 28th year -- not reaching the 28th year, he could have disposed of this contingency in his lifetime, so he predeceased its occurrence.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Do you agree to that?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I -- if I would agree to that, I might be deciding the fifth case that has to come before this Court because it's not before --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Your argument is that assuming that he could do it, still, he hasn't done it (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: He hasn't done it and I don't feel --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- into this case.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I don't want to get into the situation I feel that Fisher, if you're asking my opinion as a lawyer, I would like to answer it and not avoid Your Honor's question. I feel under the thrust of Fisher versus Witmark, one must say that that right is also assignable. I think correctly or incorrectly, the copyright bar had been working upon that assumption in carrying Fisher versus Witmark further. Now, whether they are correct and not as fort this Court to say and not for counsel here to say of what -- what you --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I think that's a very fair statement.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What you're say -- what you're saying then here is that all we have here is a case where the right time the contract was not drawn and it's probably this case, it's empower to be granted I suppose.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, the counsel --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Having been that here, a question of how you're going to construe a contract that --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: That phrase frightens me somewhat having been counsel of Hammerstein versus Superior Court shortly after leaving law school. I -- I don't think the -- the writ in improvably granted here, it is a federal question, the -- not a jurisdictional problem but perhaps this Court should not have granted hearing in this case, yes. I think, it's just a question of interpretation of contract.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It -- it doesn't require a great professional courage to take that position.[Laughter]

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I'm sorry but the question was put, I have to get --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: There's no need to worry. You should have great professional courage. You are here to win a case.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes. Well, but I have to answer that (Voice Overlap) quite frankly, Your Honor and I --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I -- I respected the whole of your argument. Let me face --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Thank you very much.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: That is -- is --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Wanted by the statement.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: May I -- is that to say that you're here, this is just to construe a contract, is that to say that you do not regard Section 24 as ambiguous?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, I might give you about 20 different ambiguities in Section 24 but not on before the Court at this time --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: That the --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: But I think that it's all ambiguous here. I think it is not ambiguous, I think it setup four classes of rights for this question in this case.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I -- the columns that I as practitioner have to face in interpreting Section 24, I would love to have this Court's advisory capacity. So --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: And down this --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- I can safely advise my client.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: More profitable for you that we don't. [Laughter]

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: And down the --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I believe so, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: I feel bound to say I agree with you. I do not think the statute is ambiguous as related to our question.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: As related from the question here whether you -- whether I am to interpret that statement as you'll agree with me, [Laugher] direct me in the dark.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And suppose one -- suppose one disagrees with your interpretation of the contract, have you any argument then?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes. I do Your Honor. I believe then that we come up to a question which should -- should really not be before this Court but which we've cited authorities below that the appellant would be bound by the judgment of the Superior Court in California which distributed the right which the executor exercise. This copyright was renewed by the executor, went into the estate. If you interpret the instrument as an agreement to leave a will then it becomes a question of state law whether it complied with those aspects of it and then under state law becomes a further question if he is not bound by the decree of distribution distributing it for the nieces and nephews. See, what happened here was, they got it from three brothers and the man quite naturally having brothers have the same ages as he was, made a general request, some specific request and left everything to his nieces and nephews, those are nephew -- nieces and nephews that took the right that was exercised by the executor. But we do get a question of state law if you want to interpret the instrument but it has not been urged. He started out urging that but when faced in the lower court unless the res judicata question departed from that and has rested this case. I submit solely, on the assignment of the author which is step one in which assignment I say never vested that right, never vested because the man died before the 28th year. Then the widow, child -- the children came in since their being not -- we went to the third step, the author's executor and as I hoped that I have now made clear and I apologize if I did not make it clear before. We're not arguing that the author during his lifetime could not assign that right but we say, the Court doesn't reach that question because he didn't assign it.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Are you -- I -- I want that clear. Do you understand that the Witmark case held that -- and you're barred from challenging it on that term that a man in his lifetime would make an assignment which, of his bar or to those claims. He died having tried to make it a -- make a will after that to give it to somebody else. And you understand that the Witmark case holds that he could not do that?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I do not so understand the Witmark case. I understand the Witmark case is deciding the facts in that case to with that the man survived the 28th year and that his assignment prior to that date was binding upon him. And the Witmark case and --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- to that regard here, go one step further with the Witmark case though, I will confess the commentators have. And it has been quoted to the proposition that you have given Mr. Justice Black.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But, I'm asking him because I -- I'm --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, I can't say, Wit --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- a little confused by quite different to my brother, I'm a little confused in that position by the statements you made. I find it a little difficult to say as the lawfulness this assignment to be made so then take effect so as to bar the executor claiming the rights. I find it a little difficult to say it has got to be in certain kind of language with reference to the appointment itself. I find that a little difficult.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, can I give you this?

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And I find it far more difficult than I would maybe it's because I just said it in the other case.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes. I'm aware of that Your Honor but --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: On the -- somewhat believe that the mere fact if the case held there could be an assignment, does not mean now that I have to say that it also deprives the man of making a will which would designate someone else to receive it. I had not understood the Witmark case to go that far, maybe it does.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, I don't think the Witmark case went so far because what I'm getting at is this. Let's say you own Black Acre, you have different incidents of an ownership of property. Let's say I don't own Black Acre but I have an easement over Black Acre for certain purposes and let's say with respect to a scream on Black Acre, I have certain riparian rights or different type of easement. So, let's just make it simple two different types of easement, two different types rights, I could well leave a will, leave the --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I have no doubt about Black Acre. I'm not saying about Black Acre.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: No. But the rights I'm trying --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I'm thinking about this statute.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: But this statute --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: It gives certain people the right in all their name to do certain things and that's all I -- the Black Acre --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I'll answer it --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- couldn't get me anywhere.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, may I attempt to answer this --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) is what I don't understand.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Mr. Justice Black, may I answer it this way. That Fisher versus Witmark dealt with the right given by Congress to the author to renew the copyright if he survived the 28th year. This case deals with the right given by Congress to an author who does not survive the 28th year and who does not live surviving him a wife, widower or children or child to bequeath by will.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Now, that I understand.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Now, Fisher versus Witmark said that the right he acquired if he lived was assignable prior to that vesting. And I simply say that if the Court feels it necessary to decide in this case that that right is also assignable here, it can do so without affecting the decision but I don't think it's necessary to the decision in the case because there was no assignment --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, Mr. Rudin.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- of that right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I put it to you that the petitions for writ came here on two questions and you accepted the questions presented so that the point, that's why it was a fresh matter for me. The point you're making or the argument you're making a while ago that assuming there could be an assignment this isn't -- but this doesn't satisfy the requirement for such an assignment, is that right?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I -- I say that that --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: It there is assignment of that right to bequeath.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes. That -- that is not open under the statutes presented by this petition with which you agreed I think in your -- in your respondent's brief in our position.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, if --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Isn't that right?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes. But if -- if the question, what is -- does the author's prior assignment defeat the rights of the widow, widower or does it defeat the rights of the executor? My answer is no.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If it's two reasons, one, it couldn't under the statute, the other is that it didn't.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I -- I respectfully submit on some (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: I gather your position is you may defend the objections in your favor below in any grounds you can without regard to the questions of --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well, certainly we did but we don't think we have to defend it because he hasn't raised it. It's brought in here on the sole issue we believe as to whether the assignment of the right in step one as I call it, defeated the exercise of the rights under step three. And we say that that's -- there are no different classes, they're all the same right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You can defend a judgement on any ground with reference to the -- to the decision of the case if you can't bring in on the questions presented, another issue not in the case.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: I don't want to make my task any harder --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- than it is, Your Honor. [Laughs]I believe that, that's it. I'd like to briefly refer to Fox versus Knowles which was cited for a proposition, I didn't understand to hold. It very simply held that the executor has the rights and renew which is independent of the author's right. It's -- it's of significance here prior to the Fox case. There seem to be the thought that the executor was in the statute only exercise the right of renewal if the author made it into the 28th year but he didn't exercise the right during the 28th year. And therefore, the executor had a limited function in the statute. In Fox case held otherwise and said that it was a separate and independent right. Referring to what is exercising the same right of the decedent, it didn't mean the same grant of right under step one, the same type of right. Just as this Court has said that the widow and children exercise the same right that the author have if he dies (Inaudible)

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: And that -- that so even though the author died prior to the beginning of the 28th year

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: And that was the situation in the Fox case. So, something did survive through the executor.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes. That's right. If something survived to the author in a way --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now, then --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- he could exercise it prior to his death, the power of appointment.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Then does an executor at least normally take assets in his hands subject to valid contracts made with the testator?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Yes. That's a true statement of general law.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now, does that apply to copyrights, I don't know?

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: It would apply to copyrights if that was the type of contract involved here, Your Honor, but it was not. If he had made a contract about the balance of the original term and the executor took it in the same manner he took Black Acre, the executor would be bound by it. But what he had, what the author had was a power of appointment and our basic position is that the petitioner here by the bargain for nor obtained the -- that right but he -- that his assignment doesn't carry with it, he didn't bargain for it when he obtained -- obtained it. He might have gotten it but perhaps if he appointed out for the man, he might have given him more than $1000 but he didn't buy that right. And it's a separate and independent right, this is a case where an executor exercises the power of appointment and it's not the question of a divulsion of property that the man own. This is after all a franchise, a grant by Congress which comes into being after the man dies and that's our position.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: It is fair to say, Mr. Rudin, if not, tell me so that the basic difference between you and -- and your brother forgetting about the terms of this particular instrument and forgetting about that -- that question. The basic difference between you is that you say that executor under the statute means executor as representative of the beneficiaries and that he says that executor under the statute means executor's representative of the person or the decedent.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Is that a -- is that a fair basic statement of the difference between --

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: It's a very short statement of it, yes.I -- I believe that sir. It's fair because -- and I think the proof of that is in the (Inaudible) case proof of support of our position. In the (Inaudible) case was, a man did not name an executor or just said, “You should go to my hometown at the carry.” The Court said he left a will and that's the intention that goes to the carry and the administrator would -- will attach, I'd like to --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Is the equivalent of an executor.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- apply that thing and turn it over to his town (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The question isn't whether you or Mr. Abeles -- or you and Mr. Abeles' view what an executor is. The question is when you use a technical term like “executor”, what lawyers who used it means by an “executor”, then you will know that the notion of an executor and what the estate is called the “universitas”. He has whatever the -- for the left. Even your case of a power of appointment is not outside of that because he has that something called he could make a disposition on his death and there is no -- there's no immaterial moment there between the death of the executor and -- between the death of the testator and the executor. He just slips into those shoes and those shoes or whatever the shoes are, I don't -- I'm not saying now, what the shoes are. It depends what the shoes are then he slips into it.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And you have to decide what the shoes are and if you change the figure if there's a -- if -- if he has something which has disposable monetary value, then he can take care of it by his will.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: My time is up and I would love to say further except I might just briefly say an executor does have rights under the Federal Employers Liability Act to cause of action.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He -- he has -- sure he has but he --

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Might I respect to state (Inaudible)

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, yes.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: -- to declare what these (Inaudible) decide that.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: No, no indeed you have more time -- yes.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: I'm sorry, counsel inadvertently said that in the Fisher case the -- the Court had mentioned the fact that the man had died and that depended upon whether or not he lived for the assignment to take effect. I respectfully --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He corrected that. (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: -- he doesn't say that all. I know nothing in a --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) and corrected that.

turns.name: MILTON A. RUDIN
turns.text: Until now you repeated again at the end perhaps --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) But he did lived the 28th years, didn't he?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Yes he did. But you see if you -- if you please, it says that the petition for certiorari -- said the petition for certiorari in this Court stated the sole question as whether an agreement to assign his renewal made by an author advanced to the 28th year, the original term of the copyright is valid and forceful. Now, (Inaudible) the Court decide that it was not. Thank you so much.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well, may I ask you what your position means that your assignment would also cutoff the next of kin had there been no will?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: The -- that assignment would but the assignment of the company were not because we obtained assignments for the next of kin. You see, we protect our self --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well, I --

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: But if I didn't get definitely so.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Suppose you had not had one, the man had died without a will.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Wouldn't be -- wouldn't -- my assignment wouldn't be effective.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Would not be good?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: No.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Why? Because certain statute says, “If he doesn't leave a will, the next of kin get it automatically.”

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And also the statute says that he makes a will it's --

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: But I still --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- his executor gets it?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: That's right. You see, if he made a will then we should get it, that's correct, because the executor as this Court said (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But the executor --

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: -- saves us to the shoes of --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And if he is -- in other words, if a man doesn't make a will, it goes to next of kin despite your assignment.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Unless I get a separate assignment.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well, unless you get from -- I mean you -- you can't overwrite the rights of the next of kin.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Of course, not.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well, that's while you were --

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: That's the reason I got it from the --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But if he does make a -- but if he does make a will, then it's no good.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: The assignment for the next of kin, correct.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well, I'm -- the -- I'm not talking about next of kin he does make a will and decides he wants to give it to the next of kin and somebody else. Then your assignment is good as against that will.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Yes. Of course you see as this Court said if I may respectfully say so, that if he has no wife and child he has a power, see, to designate by will, the executor apply for the renewal which of course would be for the purpose affecting the author's disposition thereof. That's the way it is done. If it intended, if the statute intended, may I say, that it should only be applied -- it should only apply for beneficiaries undergo it until he said so. Instead of saying, he didn't leave a will or to have to save us if I might respectfully say so, if -- if there's no beneficiaries this is -- it is not disposed at the beneficiaries. That's all just that same --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well there are many -- many ways they could have made it clear.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: That's true --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But if --

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: And I think -- I think --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I -- I understand that here you say that in the first place, it would go to the widow and the children.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Definitely.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: No assignment could cut it off?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Definitely.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: The next class they make is an executor.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: You say that that is cutoff by society.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Cutoff badly, sir?

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: No, by your assignment to you.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: No. No, I said -- well, put up with other way, if you don't mind.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well, does the -- is the will any good does -- does anybody get anything from it?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Yes. We (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: The point of assignment you speak of.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Yes we do because if it wasn't for the will, we wouldn't get it by appointing the executor, he really effectuates the disposition to us through the executor.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But even though he didn't try to.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: I don't know if he did or didn't. So, he didn't make a specific requirement.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Did he name you as -- did the will name you as the beneficiary?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: No.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Alright. Then the second class is the executor. Now, the third class which comes even after that is why they have no will?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Do you say that your assignment is no good against him?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: If there's no will, that's correct.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now then, let me ask you if I may sir.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: I want to get back to this administrator business. Do you know of any law of decent under any state in the country that does not with respect to chattels leave them to next of kin describing they may be perhaps. But do they not all do that and yet or not every such is not every such asset an asset in the hands of the administrator there being no will? Then do you still maintain that these assets if it survives and there be no will, is not an asset in the hands of the administrator?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Well, I -- the Court could leave him. I just didn't want to take that technical position, you know I'm -- because the other proposition to save so, why should I do that. I -- I feel this way, I'm absolutely --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: You have to take it in a way --

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: I am.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- to keep any inconsistency of the statute and the statute full read is perfectly plain, isn't it?

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: I understand that, sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: It's just plain language.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: That's right, sir. If you don't mind my saying.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: It is to me.

turns.name: JULIAN T. ABELES
turns.text: Thank you sir.
